questions.Tips: Session duration management for human access = IAM Identity Center. IAM Identity Center (SSO) has built-in session duration settings. For role-based sessions, aws:TokenIssueTime can enforce max session age. Password policies ≠ session timeouts. This is one reason AWS recommends migrating from IAM Users to IAM Identity Center for human access: centralized session management, SSO, and easier lifecycle control.

questions.questionText: A company is experiencing high costs from their EC2 workload. A Cost Explorer analysis shows 30% of instances have less than 5% average CPU utilization over the past 30 days. AWS Compute Optimizer recommends downsizing these instances. The company also has 40 instances running 24/7 at 70% CPU that are currently On-Demand. What two cost optimization actions should be prioritized?

options.A: Convert all 40 high-utilization instances to Spot; terminate the low-utilization instances immediately
options.B: Downsize the under-utilized instances to smaller instance types or remove them; purchase Compute Savings Plans for the 40 consistently high-utilization instances
options.C: Enable T3 Unlimited mode for all instances to maximize CPU performance
options.D: Move all instances to Reserved Instances with No Upfront payment to avoid commitment

A.description: Converting 24/7 customer-facing On-Demand instances to Spot creates interruption risk. Terminating under-utilized instances without investigation may disrupt dependent services.
A.reason: Incorrect — Spot Instances are not appropriate for 24/7 consistently utilized instances without business continuity planning. Immediate termination of 'low CPU' instances requires validating they are truly unused, not just bursty.

B.description: Right-sizing under-utilized instances (confirmed low-value resources) reduces waste. Purchasing Compute Savings Plans for consistently high-utilization On-Demand instances converts them to discounted rates (up to 66%) without changing instance type flexibility.
B.reason: Correct — this addresses both issues: waste reduction (right-sizing/removing low-utilization) and pricing optimization (Savings Plans for steady-state workloads). Both are standard AWS Well-Architected cost optimization practices.

C.description: T3 Unlimited mode removes CPU credit throttling for T3 instances by charging for extra CPU — it increases performance but also increases cost, the opposite of optimization.
C.reason: Incorrect — T3 Unlimited increases cost for CPU usage beyond the baseline; it's a performance optimization, not a cost optimization. The existing instances may not even be T3 instances.

D.description: Reserved Instances with No Upfront still commit to a 1-year term for a specific instance type; they save money versus On-Demand but are less flexible than Compute Savings Plans and still require type/size commitment.
D.reason: Incorrect — No Upfront RIs provide smaller discounts than All Upfront RIs. Compute Savings Plans provide better flexibility for the same or better discount. This option also doesn't address the under-utilized instances.

questions.Tips: Two-part cost optimization: (1) Right-size/remove low-utilization instances (eliminate waste). (2) Savings Plans for high-utilization steady-state workloads (optimize pricing). Cost optimization framework: eliminate waste FIRST, then optimize pricing on remaining resources. Savings Plans > No-Upfront RIs for flexibility. Never recommend Spot for 24/7 production without addressing interruption handling. This is the AWS Well-Architected Cost Optimization pillar in action.

questions.questionText: A company uses AWS CloudFormation to manage EC2 infrastructure. The CloudFormation service role needs permissions to create EC2 instances, Security Groups, and IAM Roles. A security engineer is concerned that the CloudFormation role could be used to create IAM Roles with more permissions than the CloudFormation role itself has, enabling privilege escalation. How should this be addressed?

options.A: Remove iam:CreateRole from the CloudFormation service role entirely
options.B: Add a Permission Boundary to all IAM Roles created by CloudFormation by including the PermissionsBoundary property in the CloudFormation IAM Role resource definition, and restrict the CloudFormation role so it can only create roles that have this boundary applied
options.C: Use CloudFormation drift detection to monitor for any unexpected IAM Role changes
options.D: Create a separate CloudFormation stack for IAM resources and restrict who can run that stack

A.description: Removing iam:CreateRole entirely prevents the CloudFormation stack from creating any IAM Roles, which may be required for the application's service roles and instance profiles.
A.reason: Incorrect — the application may legitimately need IAM Roles (e.g., EC2 instance profiles). Removing iam:CreateRole entirely breaks legitimate infrastructure-as-code functionality.

B.description: Requiring a Permission Boundary on all CloudFormation-created IAM Roles limits what those roles can do. Restricting the CloudFormation role using an IAM condition (iam:PermissionsBoundary must equal the approved boundary ARN) prevents the CloudFormation role from creating roles with more permissions than the boundary allows.
B.reason: Correct — this is the privilege escalation prevention pattern. The CloudFormation role can create roles, but all created roles must have the boundary attached (enforced by IAM condition). The boundary caps what created roles can do, preventing escalation beyond the boundary's permissions.

C.description: CloudFormation drift detection identifies stack resource changes made outside of CloudFormation; it is a detective control that runs after the fact and doesn't prevent privilege escalation.
C.reason: Incorrect — drift detection is a detective, not preventive, control. Privilege escalation could occur before drift detection identifies it.

D.description: Separating IAM stacks limits who can create IAM resources but doesn't prevent the privileged IAM stack operators from creating overly permissive roles.
D.reason: Incorrect — separating IAM into a separate stack restricts who can run it but doesn't prevent the operators of that stack from creating over-privileged roles.

questions.Tips: CloudFormation privilege escalation prevention = Permission Boundaries on created roles. Pattern: CloudFormation service role has iam:CreateRole but with condition: iam:PermissionsBoundary must equal approved boundary ARN. All created IAM Roles get the boundary applied (required by CloudFormation template). This ensures no CloudFormation-created role can have more permissions than the boundary allows. This is the standard IaC privilege escalation prevention pattern.

questions.questionText: A company has an EC2 instance running a web server on port 80. The instance is in a public subnet with an Elastic IP. Users cannot access the website from the internet. The VPC has an Internet Gateway attached. The instance's Security Group allows inbound TCP 80 from 0.0.0.0/0. What else must be checked?

options.A: The EC2 instance IAM Role must allow HTTP traffic
options.B: The route table associated with the public subnet must have a route for 0.0.0.0/0 pointing to the Internet Gateway
options.C: The EBS volume attached to the instance must have read permissions enabled
options.D: The EC2 instance must have Enhanced Networking enabled to handle public traffic

A.description: IAM Roles on EC2 instances grant permissions to call AWS APIs (S3, DynamoDB, etc.); they have no role in allowing or blocking inbound HTTP traffic from the internet.
A.reason: Incorrect — IAM Roles control AWS API authorization, not network traffic. Web traffic from users is handled by VPC routing and Security Groups, not IAM.

B.description: For internet traffic to reach a public subnet instance, the subnet's route table must have a route for all internet traffic (0.0.0.0/0) pointing to the Internet Gateway. Without this route, packets from the internet cannot reach the instance even though it has a public IP.
B.reason: Correct — the Internet Gateway is attached but the route table connection is the missing link. The packet flow is: Internet → IGW → route table lookup → subnet → Security Group check → instance. The route table entry is required.

C.description: EBS volume read permissions are OS-level filesystem permissions that control which users on the instance can read the disk; they have no effect on incoming network traffic.
C.reason: Incorrect — EBS volume permissions are about OS-level disk access, not network accessibility. The web server can't be reached over the network regardless of disk permissions.

D.description: Enhanced Networking improves network throughput and reduces latency for high-bandwidth applications; it is not required for basic public internet accessibility.
D.reason: Incorrect — Enhanced Networking is a performance optimization for high-bandwidth workloads, not a requirement for basic internet accessibility. Standard networking supports public HTTP traffic.

questions.Tips: Internet connectivity checklist for EC2: (1) Internet Gateway attached to VPC ✓, (2) Route table has 0.0.0.0/0 → IGW (CHECK!), (3) Public IP or Elastic IP ✓, (4) Security Group inbound rule ✓, (5) NACL allows traffic (if customized). The route table is the most commonly missed piece. A subnet is 'public' only when its route table has the IGW route — without it, the subnet is private regardless of IGW attachment.

questions.questionText: A company stores application logs on EC2 instance EBS volumes. The logs grow significantly over time, and the company wants to analyze logs from the past 2 years using Amazon Athena. Current EBS volume costs are high. What is the MOST cost-effective long-term storage solution for these logs?

options.A: Increase EBS volume sizes to accommodate 2 years of logs and use EBS Snapshots for backup
options.B: Ship logs to Amazon S3 using the CloudWatch Logs agent or custom scripts; configure S3 Intelligent-Tiering or lifecycle policies to transition older logs to S3 Glacier; query with Athena directly from S3
options.C: Store logs on additional EC2 instance store volumes for faster I/O performance
options.D: Create an Amazon EFS file system and mount it to all EC2 instances for centralized log storage

A.description: EBS volumes are expensive block storage ($0.08/GB for gp3) designed for low-latency, random I/O workloads; using EBS for 2 years of log archives is significantly more expensive than S3 ($0.023/GB standard).
A.reason: Incorrect — EBS is 3-4x more expensive than S3 for the same data volume and is not designed for archival storage. EBS Snapshots are cheaper than EBS volumes but more expensive than S3 lifecycle tiers.

B.description: Amazon S3 is designed for large-scale object storage at low cost. CloudWatch Logs agent streams logs to S3 or CloudWatch Logs. S3 Intelligent-Tiering automatically moves objects between access tiers. Athena queries S3 directly, making this the most cost-effective analytics-enabled log storage solution.
B.reason: Correct — S3 + Athena is the purpose-built AWS solution for log analytics at scale. S3 costs are a fraction of EBS; Athena charges per-query (per TB scanned); lifecycle policies or Intelligent-Tiering reduce older log costs further.

C.description: Instance store volumes are ephemeral (data lost on stop/termination) and physically tied to specific instances; they are completely unsuitable for long-term, durable log storage.
C.reason: Incorrect — instance store is the worst possible choice for durable long-term storage. All data is permanently lost when the instance stops or is terminated.

D.description: EFS (Elastic File System) is a shared NFS file system with costs similar to or higher than gp2 EBS (approximately $0.30/GB standard); it is more expensive than S3 and not optimized for log analytics workloads.
D.reason: Incorrect — EFS is more expensive than S3 for archival data and doesn't offer native query capabilities like Athena's S3 integration. EFS is designed for shared POSIX workloads, not log archival.

questions.Tips: Log storage + Athena analytics = S3. S3 is orders of magnitude cheaper than EBS/EFS for large data volumes. Cost comparison per GB: EFS Standard ~$0.30 > EBS gp3 ~$0.08 > S3 Standard ~$0.023 > S3-IA ~$0.0125 > S3 Glacier ~$0.004. Athena queries S3 directly — it was designed for this pattern. Add S3 lifecycle rules to move logs to cheaper tiers as they age: Standard → IA (after 30 days) → Glacier (after 90 days).

questions.questionText: A company runs EC2 instances that must be patched monthly. The operations team currently patches instances manually by SSHing into each one. They want to automate patching with zero manual effort, ensure only approved patches are applied, and maintain a compliance report. Which AWS service and configuration should the architect recommend?

options.A: Create a cron job on each instance that runs yum update monthly
options.B: Use AWS Systems Manager Patch Manager with a patch baseline defining approved patches, a maintenance window for the monthly schedule, and a patch compliance report in Systems Manager
options.C: Use AWS Config to detect unpatched instances and send notifications to the operations team
options.D: Create an AMI with all patches applied monthly and use instance refresh to replace all running instances

A.description: Cron-based yum update applies all available patches without approval control, doesn't integrate with compliance reporting, and requires manual deployment to each instance.
A.reason: Incorrect — cron-based patching provides no patch approval workflow, no centralized reporting, and no scheduling management. It also requires per-instance configuration.

B.description: Systems Manager Patch Manager provides: (1) Patch Baselines to define approved patches with approval delays, (2) Maintenance Windows for scheduled patching, (3) Patch Groups to target instance fleets, and (4) Patch compliance dashboards.
B.reason: Correct — Patch Manager is the purpose-built AWS service for automated, policy-driven patching with compliance reporting. It uses SSM Agent (no SSH required), supports approval workflows, and generates compliance reports automatically.

C.description: AWS Config detects configuration compliance but does not apply patches; notifying the operations team still requires manual patching, not automation.
C.reason: Incorrect — Config is a detective control for configuration compliance. It doesn't perform patching actions; it only identifies and alerts on violations.

D.description: Monthly AMI baking and instance refresh is a valid immutable infrastructure approach but results in complete instance replacement (new instance ID, IP, etc.) for what should be a patch operation, and is operationally complex.
D.reason: Incorrect — while AMI-based patching is a valid pattern, it is significantly more disruptive (replaces instances entirely) and complex than in-place patching with Patch Manager for a monthly patch cycle.

questions.Tips: Automated patching + approved patches + compliance reports = AWS Systems Manager Patch Manager. Components: (1) Patch Baseline (which patches are approved), (2) Patch Group (which instances to patch, via tags), (3) Maintenance Window (when to patch), (4) SSM Run Command/State Manager (executes patching), (5) Patch Compliance Dashboard (reporting). No SSH required — uses SSM Agent. This is one of the most commonly tested SSM features on the SAA exam.

questions.questionText: A company has a production EC2 instance that is unresponsive. The SSM Agent on the instance is not responding to session manager. SSH on port 22 is closed (no security group rule). The operations team needs to access the instance to diagnose the problem. What is the FASTEST way to gain access?

options.A: Create a new Security Group rule opening port 22 and SSH using the key pair
options.B: Use EC2 Serial Console to connect to the instance's serial port, which provides out-of-band access independent of the network stack and SSM Agent
options.C: Stop the instance, modify it to a different instance type, and restart to reset the network
options.D: Take an EBS snapshot, create a new instance from the snapshot, and diagnose from there

A.description: Adding a Security Group rule for port 22 and SSHing requires the instance's network stack to be functional and the SSH daemon to be running. If the instance is unresponsive due to a network or OS issue, SSH would also fail.
A.reason: Incorrect — if the instance is unresponsive (potentially kernel panic, OS crash, or network stack failure), adding an SSH Security Group rule won't help if the underlying issue is in the OS or network stack.

B.description: EC2 Serial Console provides access to the instance through its serial port (via the BIOS/console), bypassing the network stack entirely. This works even when the OS is in a bad state, the network is down, or the SSH daemon has crashed.
B.reason: Correct — the Serial Console is out-of-band access that works independently of the operating system's network stack. It's the AWS equivalent of having physical keyboard and monitor access to a server.

C.description: Stopping and restarting the instance moves it to new hardware, which might resolve hardware-level issues, but stopping a production instance that is unresponsive may cause data loss and is not diagnostic in nature.
C.reason: Incorrect — changing instance type requires a stop/start cycle which doesn't provide diagnostic access and could lose in-memory state or exacerbate the issue.

D.description: Creating a new instance from an EBS snapshot would allow examining the disk contents but is not 'the fastest' approach; it takes significant time and doesn't allow diagnosis of the live running instance state.
D.reason: Incorrect — snapshot-based forensics is a post-mortem technique, not a live diagnosis approach. It also takes 10-30+ minutes to complete.

questions.Tips: EC2 Serial Console = out-of-band emergency access when network and SSM are unavailable. Use when: OS crashed, network stack failed, SSH daemon not running, kernel panic, startup issues. Requirements: must be enabled at account level AND instance type must support it (most current generation types). Serial Console provides direct access to the instance's serial port — equivalent to physical console access in a data center.

questions.questionText: A company needs to ensure their EC2 Auto Scaling Group maintains exactly 6 instances across 3 Availability Zones (2 per AZ) at all times, regardless of traffic. If an instance in one AZ becomes unhealthy, a replacement should be launched in the SAME AZ to maintain the 2-per-AZ distribution. How should the ASG be configured?

options.A: Set min=6, max=6, desired=6; configure health checks; the ASG will automatically rebalance to maintain AZ distribution
options.B: Create a separate Auto Scaling Group per Availability Zone with min=2, max=2, desired=2 in each
options.C: Set min=6, max=6, desired=6 with the ASG configured across all 3 AZs; the ASG uses AZ rebalancing by default to maintain even distribution; a replacement is launched in the same AZ as the terminated instance
options.D: Use EC2 Auto Recovery instead of an ASG to recover failed instances in the same AZ

A.description: min=max=desired=6 keeps capacity fixed. However, the question asks specifically about maintaining 2-per-AZ and launching replacements in the same AZ — these are behaviors that require the ASG to be configured across the 3 AZs, which is what Option C clarifies.
A.reason: Partially correct but incomplete — this option is true but doesn't clarify the multi-AZ behavior. Option C is more precisely correct as it explains the default AZ rebalancing behavior.

B.description: Three separate ASGs per AZ work but create management overhead; the ASGs cannot coordinate capacity decisions, and standard features like capacity rebalancing across AZs would not be available.
B.reason: Incorrect — separate per-AZ ASGs create operational complexity. A single ASG spanning multiple AZs is the standard approach; AWS handles AZ distribution automatically.

C.description: An ASG configured with all 3 AZs and min=max=desired=6 maintains fixed capacity. ASG AZ rebalancing keeps 2 instances per AZ. When an instance is terminated for health, the ASG launches a replacement in the same AZ first, maintaining the per-AZ balance.
C.reason: Correct — a single multi-AZ ASG with fixed capacity automatically handles AZ distribution. When replacing a failed instance, the ASG replaces it in the same AZ to restore balance. This is the standard, managed approach.

D.description: EC2 Auto Recovery recovers the specific instance on the same hardware within the same AZ, which maintains AZ placement. However, Auto Recovery is for hardware-level failures only and doesn't work for application-level health check failures.
D.reason: Incorrect — Auto Recovery handles only system status check failures (host hardware). Application-level failures (unhealthy HTTP responses) require ASG health checks and replacement. An ASG is needed for application health monitoring.

questions.Tips: Fixed capacity across AZs = min=max=desired + multi-AZ ASG. The ASG automatically distributes instances evenly across configured AZs and replaces unhealthy instances in the same AZ. Three separate ASGs is operationally complex and non-standard. EC2 Auto Recovery handles hardware failures only. The ASG is the right tool for application-level health-based replacement with AZ distribution.

questions.questionText: A company is deploying a new application with strict requirements: all data at rest must use customer-managed KMS keys (CMK), all data in transit must use TLS 1.2+, and all API calls to the application's EC2 instances must be logged. The instances are behind an Application Load Balancer. Which combination FULLY addresses all three requirements?

options.A: EBS volumes encrypted with AWS default keys; HTTP listener on ALB; CloudWatch Logs on EC2 instances
options.B: EBS volumes encrypted with CMK; HTTPS listener on ALB with a TLS 1.2+ security policy; VPC Flow Logs for API call logging
options.C: EBS volumes encrypted with CMK; HTTPS listener on ALB with a TLS 1.2+ security policy (e.g., ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-2021-06); application-level logging to CloudWatch Logs or ALB access logs for API call tracking
options.D: Instance store volumes (inherently unreadable when detached); HTTP between ALB and instances (encrypted at edge only); AWS Config for API logging

A.description: Default AWS-managed keys don't meet the customer-managed key requirement; HTTP (not HTTPS) violates TLS in-transit requirement; CloudWatch Logs on instances doesn't log API calls at the ALB level.
A.reason: Incorrect — fails all three requirements: default key ≠ CMK, HTTP ≠ TLS 1.2+, and CloudWatch Logs on instances captures application logs, not necessarily all API calls.

B.description: CMK for EBS ✓, HTTPS with TLS 1.2 ✓, but VPC Flow Logs capture network metadata (IPs, ports, bytes) not actual API call details (request paths, parameters, response codes).
B.reason: Incorrect — VPC Flow Logs record network flow metadata, not HTTP API call details. ALB access logs or application-level logging is needed for API call tracking.

C.description: CMK-encrypted EBS volumes satisfy the customer-managed key requirement. HTTPS with a TLS 1.2+ security policy on the ALB enforces TLS 1.2 minimum. ALB access logs capture every request (method, URL, response code, client IP) and application logs can supplement with business-level detail.
C.reason: Correct — this addresses all three: CMK for data at rest, TLS 1.2+ ALB policy for data in transit, and ALB access logs for API call logging. ALB access logs are the authoritative record of all requests reaching the application.

D.description: Instance store is ephemeral and not encrypted with KMS CMK; HTTP between ALB and instances transmits unencrypted on the internal network; AWS Config tracks resource configuration, not HTTP API calls.
D.reason: Incorrect — instance store doesn't use KMS, HTTP is unencrypted internally, and Config doesn't log HTTP API calls.

questions.Tips: Three requirements checklist: (1) Data at rest: EBS with CMK (not default AWS key). (2) Data in transit: HTTPS with TLS policy specifying minimum TLS 1.2+ (ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-2021-06 is the recommended policy). (3) API call logging: ALB access logs (captures all HTTP requests) or CloudTrail (captures AWS API calls). VPC Flow Logs = network metadata only. Config = resource configuration changes. Know the difference between these logging services.
